title: The Carrierï؟½s Story or, Brightenï؟½s Sister-In-Law
content:
آ آ آ آ At a point where the old road crosses
آ آ آ آ The river, and turns to the right,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d camped with the team; and the hosses
آ آ آ آ Was all fixed up for the night.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d been to the town to carry
آ آ آ آ A load to the Cudgegong;
آ آ آ آ And Iï؟½d taken the youngster, Harry,
آ آ آ آ On a trip as Iï؟½d promisï؟½d him long.

آ آ آ آ I had seven more, and another
آ آ آ آ That died at the age of three;
آ آ آ آ But they all took arter the mother,
آ آ آ آ And Harry took arter me.
آ آ آ آ And from the tiniest laddie
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas always his fondest dream
آ آ آ آ To go on the roads with his daddy,
آ آ آ آ And help him to drive the team.

آ آ آ آ He was bright at the school and clever,
آ آ آ آ The best of the youngsters there;
آ آ آ آ And the teacher said there was never
آ آ آ آ A lad that promised so fair.
آ آ آ آ And I half forgot lifeï؟½s battle,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ its long, hard-beaten road,
آ آ آ آ In the sound of the youngsterï؟½s prattle
آ آ آ آ From his perch on top oï؟½ the load.

آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ when he was tired oï؟½ ridinï؟½
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d lift him down for a walk,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½d say, at my silence chidinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Now, daddy tell me some talk.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And oft by the camp-fire sittinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ When the bush was round us wild,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d yarn by the hour, forgittinï؟½
آ آ آ آ That Harry was only a child.

آ آ آ آ But to-day heï؟½d been strange and quiet,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ lay on the chaff-bags still;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ though heï؟½d bravely deny it,
آ آ آ آ I knowï؟½d as the boy was ill.
آ آ آ آ He said he was ï؟½only doseyï؟½,
آ آ آ آ In his queer old-fashioned way;
آ آ آ آ And I fixed him up warm anï؟½ cosey
آ آ آ آ In the hammock under the dray.

آ آ آ آ I fried him some eggs and some bakinï؟½
آ آ آ آ Which I couldnï؟½t git him to touch;
آ آ آ آ And it set my heart a-achin
آ آ آ آ For heï؟½d always eaten so much.
آ آ آ آ I wandered about half silly,
آ آ آ آ And thought that my heart would stop;
آ آ آ آ And the tea got cold in the billy,
آ آ آ آ For I couldnï؟½t ï؟½aï؟½ tasted a drop.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d seen the same sickness ofï؟½en;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ my spirits began to droop,
آ آ آ آ For as soon as he started coughinï؟½
آ آ آ آ I knowï؟½d as heï؟½d got the croup.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas fifteen mile to the river;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ Gulgong was twenty-five;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I thought ï؟½twas a chance if ever
آ آ آ آ I got him back home alive.

آ آ آ آ The thought of the loss was horrid
آ آ آ آ If the young ï؟½un was taken away;
آ آ آ آ And I went and leaned my forehead
آ آ آ آ Against the tire oï؟½ the dray.
آ آ آ آ And sudden I started cryinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And sobbed like a woman too;
آ آ آ آ For I felt that the boy was dyinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And I didnï؟½t know what to do.

آ آ آ آ All helpless I was, and lonely;
آ آ آ آ But I thought ï؟½twas a cowardï؟½s cry
آ آ آ آ To call on the Saviour only
آ آ آ آ When trouble or death was nigh.
آ آ آ آ But after a while I lifted
آ آ آ آ My eyes to the steely blue
آ آ آ آ Of the sky where somethinï؟½ drifted
آ آ آ آ Like a great white cockatoo.

آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ nearer it came, and nearer,
آ آ آ آ Right down to the branch of the tree;
آ آ آ آ And it seemed when its shape grew clearer,
آ آ آ آ Like the form of a woman to me.
آ آ آ آ For a moment it seemed to tarry,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ pï؟½int away up the road,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ then seemed pintinï؟½ at Harry,
آ آ آ آ A-coughinï؟½ beneath the load.

آ آ آ آ I donï؟½t want ter arger; thereï؟½s chances
آ آ آ آ The vision was only the sky,
آ آ آ آ Or the smoke outlinï؟½d on the branches,
آ آ آ آ Or a lonely cloud on high.
آ آ آ آ But I says ï؟½twas a message from glory;
آ آ آ آ I sees as yer goinï؟½ to chaff;
آ آ آ آ Just wait till I done my story,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ laugh if yer want to laugh.

آ آ آ آ Away went the vision flyinï؟½;
آ آ آ آ Up into the blue it went;
آ آ آ آ And I stood for a minute tryinï؟½
آ آ آ آ To think what its cominï؟½ meant.
آ آ آ آ When it flashed on my brain like lightninï؟½;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ arter I thought it strange
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d almost forgotten old Brighten
آ آ آ آ Who lived on the top of the range.

آ آ آ آ He lived on a small selection,
آ آ آ آ Or used ter live there I knowï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ it lay in a west direction,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Bout five miles back from the road.
آ آ آ آ I harnessed the horses quicker
آ آ آ آ Than ever Iï؟½d taken ï؟½em out;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ they must ï؟½aï؟½ thought me in liquor,
آ آ آ آ For the way as I shovï؟½d ï؟½em about.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d allers bin fond oï؟½ sneerinï؟½
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ laughinï؟½ at womenï؟½s ways;
آ آ آ آ I could see in their lives, Iï؟½m fearinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ But little as called for praise;
آ آ آ آ But now when I thought heï؟½d smother
آ آ آ آ With croup in the lonely wild,
آ آ آ آ Good God, how I longed for a mother
آ آ آ آ To save the life of my child!

آ آ آ آ I seed in a vision each minit
آ آ آ آ The youngster nursed back into life;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ the hand of a woman was in it;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ the woman was Brightenï؟½s wife.
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s times when not knowinï؟½ a bliss is,
آ آ آ آ As Harryï؟½s school-teacher ï؟½ud say:
آ آ آ آ And I didnï؟½t know Brightenï؟½s missis
آ آ آ آ Had gone to the town that day.

آ آ آ آ In a moment Iï؟½d lifted Harry
آ آ آ آ To the bags on top of the load;
آ آ آ آ And I flogged the weary horses
آ آ آ آ Along on the dusty road.
آ آ آ آ But evï؟½rything seemï؟½d to hinder
آ آ آ آ My hopes when I reached the hut;
آ آ آ آ For there wasnï؟½t a light in the winder;
آ آ آ آ And both oï؟½ the doors was shut.

آ آ آ آ That moment my heart got hurted;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I felt it for many a day;
آ آ آ آ For I thought that the place was deserted,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ Brighten had gone away.
آ آ آ آ But I called; and the door was opened,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I saw that the hut was alight;
آ آ آ آ It hadnï؟½t shone in the winders;
آ آ آ آ For the moon was shininï؟½ bright.

آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ there in the door, with a candle,
آ آ آ آ I saw old Brighten stand,
آ آ آ آ With his fingers grasping the handle
آ آ آ آ Of a pistol he held in his hand.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½If any one moves,ï؟½ he shouted,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Iï؟½ll fire if Iï؟½ve got to hang!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ For the moment he never doubted
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas a visit from Gardï؟½nerï؟½s gang.

آ آ آ آ I didnï؟½t move in a hurry;
آ آ آ آ For a man in a fright shoots quick.
آ آ آ آ But I told him he neednï؟½t flurry,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas only a youngster sick.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Stanï؟½ back,ï؟½ said old Brighten, snatchinï؟½
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ shuttinï؟½ the door in his fright;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Itï؟½s typhoid, maybe, heï؟½s catchinï؟½:
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I canï؟½t have him here to-night.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ But a womanï؟½s voice shouted, ï؟½What is it?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d never seen her before;
آ آ آ آ She was only there on a visit;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas Brightenï؟½s sister-in-law.
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ nothinï؟½ seemed able to frighten
آ آ آ آ This woman so pale anï؟½ thin;
آ آ آ آ She pushed from the door old Brighten,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ carried the youngster in.

آ آ آ آ Sheï؟½d bin hospital nurse in the city,
آ آ آ آ I heard, and had got the sack
آ آ آ آ For havinï؟½ a little pity,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ exposinï؟½ a doctor quack;
آ آ آ آ Some trumped-up stories agin her
آ آ آ آ All over the town was belled;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ in spite of the fightinï؟½ in her
آ آ آ آ They got her at last expelled.

آ آ آ آ Anï؟½, talkinï؟½ oï؟½ fight, Iï؟½m fearinï؟½
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s sudden fightinï؟½ in store
آ آ آ آ For the first as speaks in my hearinï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Gin Brightenï؟½s sister-in-law;
آ آ آ آ For, in spite of old Brightenï؟½s cussinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ She got the youngster to bed;
آ آ آ آ And arter a weekï؟½s good nussinï؟½
آ آ آ آ She won him back from the dead.

آ آ آ آ And then I began to hanker
آ آ آ آ For a speech to tell her the joy
آ آ آ آ I felt in my heart, and to thank her
آ آ آ آ For givinï؟½ me back my boy.
آ آ آ آ The morninï؟½ I left old Brightenï؟½s,
آ آ آ آ While puttinï؟½ the horses to,
آ آ آ آ I puzzled my brains to make up
آ آ آ آ A speech as I thought would do.

آ آ آ آ She lifted the youngster and kissed him,
آ آ آ آ And helped him into the dray;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I thought of how Iï؟½d ï؟½aï؟½ missed him,
آ آ آ آ If heï؟½d only been taken away.
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½, ï؟½Mum,ï؟½ I sez; ï؟½I oughter,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ to finish the speech I tries;
آ آ آ آ But all on a sudden the water
آ آ آ آ Kem bubblinï؟½ up to my eyes.

آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ downï؟½ard, like water-courses,
آ آ آ آ The tears began to tear;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I had to swear at the horses
آ آ آ آ To hide my weakness from her.
آ آ آ آ But the tears was only human
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ they seemï؟½d to haï؟½ done some good;
آ آ آ آ For she pressed my hand like a woman,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ said that she understood.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
